steps.title: Track the author's correction
steps.content: The passage challenges the familiar reputation of Smith's invisible hand metaphor. It first describes the common view that the metaphor offers a broad model of individual economic interests producing aggregate benefits. Then the word putatively signals that the passage is questioning that common view.

steps.title: Use the historical details
steps.content: According to the passage, Smith used the metaphor only once in his economic writings and only to make a narrow point about mercantilism. The metaphor was also largely ignored until some twentieth-century economists elevated it because they wanted an intellectual pedigree for their own views.

steps.title: Reject overstatements
steps.content: Choice A accepts the later robust-model reading instead of treating it skeptically. Choice B adds unsupported approval of the metaphor's usefulness. Choice C mentions mercantilism, but it turns the narrow point into the main idea and misses the emphasis on later economists' promotion. The best summary says the metaphor's reputation comes from later promotion, not from its importance in Smith's work. The answer is D.
